title: System memory report with top 10 consuming processes
command:
echo -e "Memory Report:
$(free -h)

Top 10 Memory Consuming Processes (%MEM):
$(ps -eo pid,comm,%mem --sort=-%mem | head -n 11)"
description: Displays system memory usage in human-readable format and lists the top 10 memory-consuming processes by percentage of memory used.
explanation: The command uses 'free -h' to show memory statistics in human-readable units (e.g., MB, GB). It then uses 'ps' to list all processes sorted by memory usage (%MEM) in descending order, showing the top 10 (including header). The output is formatted with clear section headers and spacing for readability.
author: Kilo Assistant

examples.command:
echo -e "Memory Report:
$(free -h)

Top 10 Memory Consuming Processes (%MEM):
$(ps -eo pid,comm,%mem --sort=-%mem | head -n 11)"
examples.description: Run the memory report oneliner directly in the shell

examples.command:
alias memreport='echo -e "Memory Report:
$(free -h)

Top 10 Memory Consuming Processes (%MEM):
$(ps -eo pid,comm,%mem --sort=-%mem | head -n 11)"' && memreport
examples.description: Define as an alias and then execute it

output:
Memory Report:
              total        used        free      shared  buff/cache   available
Mem:           7.7G        2.3G        3.1G        256M        2.3G        5.0G
Swap:          2.0G          0B        2.0G

Top 10 Memory Consuming Processes (%MEM):
  PID COMMAND         %MEM
 1234 firefox         15.2
 5678 chrome          12.8
 9012 code             8.5
 3456 thunderbird      6.2
 7890 slack            4.1
 1122 python3          3.8
 3344 java             3.5
 5566 docker           2.9
 7788 Xorg             2.5
 9900 gnome-shell      2.2
notes: The %MEM column shows the percentage of available physical memory used by the process.
The header line (PID COMMAND %MEM) is included in the top 10 output.
For a more detailed view including memory in MB, use 'ps -eo pid,comm,rss --sort=-rss' and convert RSS from KB to MB.
